policy_statement: Implement communication circuits to transmit information to health authorities following forecasting and warning systems.
mechanism_description: Implements communication circuits so forecasting and warning information is transmitted to health authorities, improving preparedness and response capacity for climate-related health risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard forecast/early warning → improved communication & preparedness → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ climate-related health impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: Establishes and institutionalizes communication circuits/protocols that route forecast and warning information (heat alerts, air quality alerts, flood warnings, disease risk signals) to health authorities and relevant responders with clear triggers and responsibilities.  
(2) Mediating process: Earlier situational awareness and coordinated operational response (risk communications to the public, activation of cooling centers, staffing surge, outreach to vulnerable groups, emergency medical readiness, coordination with social services).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced morbidity and mortality and reduced strain on health systems during climate-related extreme events and longer-term climate-sensitive health risks.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced illness and mortality by enabling earlier and more effective public-health actions in response to climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Uses climate forecasting and warning information to trigger health-sector preparedness/response actions, reducing harm from climate hazards (a hazard → exposure/vulnerability reduction pathway).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While better coordination can yield co-benefits (e.g., efficiency in service delivery), the core and largest climate-relevant effect is protection from climate impacts, not emissions reduction (Mitigation), reduced resource inputs (Resource Efficiency), or ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If an early warning system targeted emissions-intensive behaviors (e.g., grid carbon signals) it could be mitigation-adjacent, but this mechanism is explicitly health-risk response capacity tied to climate hazards, keeping it Adaptation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that creates/strengthens communication circuits from climate early-warning/forecast systems to health authorities is fundamentally aimed at reducing climate-related health impacts through preparedness and response.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., heatwave vs. flood vs. air quality; governance capacity vs. public health operations) require policy-level details and are not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could include minor mitigation co-benefits (e.g., avoided emergency generator use through better planning), but the dominant and intended pathway is health impact reduction via preparedness/response, so classification remains Adaptation.
key_indicators: “Implementation of communication circuits”; “transmission of information to health authorities”; “following forecasting and warning systems”
co_benefits: Improved routine public health surveillance/incident management beyond climate events; stronger emergency preparedness for non-climate hazards.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as an infrastructure/ICT investment if the “communication circuits” are primarily technical systems rather than protocols; the text does not specify funding or technology deployment, so classified as planning/strategy.
